mappings.rationaleAI: Australia's interwar decline of Labor Party and Great Depression (B.1), post-1945 political and economic developments including Menzies and Whitlam (B.2), international relations including Korean and Vietnam wars and China recognition (B.3), and social developments including Aboriginal rights and immigration (B.4).
Oceania's post-WWI mandate system (A.1), WWII in the Pacific and effects on indigenous peoples (A.2), and post-WWII trusteeship administrations and independent island states (A.3).
WWII in the Pacific covering Japanese power's rise and fall and effects on indigenous peoples (A.2), and Australia's military role and economic effects (B.1).
Post-WWII movements toward autonomy among indigenous peoples and independent island states (A.3), and independence of Papua New Guinea (B.3).
Australia's Commonwealth membership and post-war relations with Britain connect to political and economic developments (B.2).
The Great Depression's impact on Australia's economy and formation of the United Australia Party (B.1) and New Zealand's Great Depression experience (C.1).
Australian participation in regional security alliances and military involvement in the Korean and Vietnam wars (B.3) were Cold War engagements.
The UN trusteeship system's role in post-WWII reorganization and the dissolution of the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (A.3) are directly covered.
Australian opposition to French nuclear testing in the South Pacific is directly covered under international relations (B.3).
The post-WWI League of Nations mandate system administering former colonial territories in Oceania is directly covered (A.1).
Australia's military collaboration with the US in the Korean and Vietnam Wars and regional security alliances (B.3).
The post-WWI situation of occupying powers in Oceania and the League of Nations mandate system represent continued imperial control (A.1).
